metric_value: $2.6 billion in 1996 to $220+ billion by 2001
notes: Aberdeen estimate; web buyers percentage climbing from 25% to 39% in same period; nearly half of all web users will come from outside US in 3 years

metric_value: $8 billion in 1997 to $327 billion in 2002
notes: Cited as factor propelling E-commerce adoption; World Bank cited on infrastructure improvements

metric_value: Less than 15% of users within an organization
notes: Via licensing; web-enabling ERP will cause this number to skyrocket as customers and business partners gain access

metric_value: PC home penetration still less than 50%
notes: Rising fairly quickly with recent advent of sub-$1000 complete PCs; cited as limiting factor for consumer EC

metric_value: IS decision-makers receive hundreds of calls yearly from professional services providers; it is a buyers market
notes: Aberdeen cautions that marketing of services sometimes outstrips actual service resources; buyers must verify trained manpower

metric_name: E-commerce professional services consulting billing rates
metric_value: $2000 to $5000 per day per consultant for strategy and process reengineering; $750 to $3000 per day for implementation and support
notes: IS executives observe that consultants cost lots of money

metric_value: One to two years away from one-stop shop EC implementation; today individual suppliers provide 80-90% of required functionality
notes: As recently as one year prior landscape required tying together packages from multiple suppliers; significant customization will remain even with complete solutions

metric_value: Product immaturity; inexperience at professional services level; lack of standards; legacy integration issues; fear of disclosure; cost barriers
notes: Based on IS manager interviews during Aberdeen research

metric_value: E-commerce systems should be considered to last at best only a few years; highly dynamic entity
notes: Professional services firms must provide ongoing lifecycle management as well as deployment services

metric_name: EDI-origin enterprise advantage in internet EC
metric_value: Enterprises with EDI origin generally quicker to understand internet EC due to legacy/systems integration experience
notes: Aberdeen observation; system-to-system integration lies at heart of maximizing ROI

metric_name: EC professional services skill requirements ranked
metric_value: Infrastructure skills foremost; then architectural design; then installation; then integration/customization; then EDI/security/benchmarking
notes: Based on IS manager feedback; finding experts with direct CAP experience may be toughest challenge; availability of experts may influence platform choice

metric_value: He who lingers is lost; companies hesitating to adopt full-blown EC strategy may find themselves trailing competitors
notes: Key Aberdeen finding; hesitation leaves enterprise trailing not-so-hesitant competitors

metric_value: Internet threatens to outflank highly profitable VANs; EDI/VAN integrators must embrace internet to survive
notes: EDI providers have big head start; but Web threatens to outflank VAN revenue model

metric_name: ERP as best enterprise EC entry point
metric_value: ERP providers have the best way in to the enterprise for EC services
notes: Key Aberdeen finding; ERP entrenches EC at enterprise core; SAP via R/3 installations best positioned

metric_value: 120000 professionals in Global Services; 10000 dedicated to E-business
notes: Works almost exclusively with World 2000; gap in midrange and smaller enterprise segments; likely to remain overall champion in EC professional services

metric_value: $6.2 billion
notes: Second behind IBM ($20B) and just ahead of Digital ($5.8B); responsible for over 50% of all commercial Unix web servers

metric_value: $5.4 billion; 40% of company revenue
notes: $13B total revenue; number three OEM in professional services; Compaq acquiring for services and sales force; combined Compaq-Tandem-Digital is major EC force

metric_value: 10000+ consultants in 55 countries; $5.6B in sales
notes: Most service professionals of any ISV; cross-product leverage; energizing applications market gives Oracle best chance to ride EC wave

metric_value: Running in 7000+ companies worldwide including Microsoft IBM and General Motors
notes: Consulting = nearly one-third of Q4-97 revenue; Accelerated SAP (ASAP) targets middle market; best source for EC for SAP-installed enterprises

metric_value: Joint venture with Intel (Pandesic LLC) announced August 1997 to bring SAP applications to mid-range and small enterprises
notes: 60-70% of Pandesic customers in small or very small range; SAP EC face also via Commerce One and Aspect Development integration with R/3

metric_value: 70% of new business internet-related in 1997; up from 50% in 1996
notes: 3000-strong staff; fixed-price contracts differentiate from time-and-materials competitors; focuses on Baan and PeopleSoft not SAP

metric_value: E-commerce only 2% of revenues in 1997
notes: 110000 employees in 44 countries; A.T. Kearney $1.1B 1997; major InterWorld E-business project March 1998; Aberdeen questions EC experience level and commitment

metric_value: 2000 service professionals dedicated to E-commerce; only IBM claims more
notes: E-commerce provider for nearly 30 years; 40000+ largest customers; 42 countries; substantial financial resources; GE parent provides captive client base

metric_value: Number two EDI/VAN integrator; most progressive in moving to Internet/EDI; second in size only to GEIS
notes: Broadest product offerings in industry; vision of combined VAN/EDI and internet channels consistent with Aberdeen analysis; ahead of GEIS and Harbinger but not IBM

metric_name: Harbinger employee count and market position
metric_value: Fewer than 1000 employees; baby among big three EDI/VAN integrators; lead in PC-based EDI
notes: Premenos acquisition makes Harbinger largest Internet-based EDI provider for AS/400; midrange EC within grasp; positioned to undercut larger competition

metric_value: Microsoft is not intent on becoming a major market force in professional services; will stick to selling software licenses
notes: Only company shipping all major EC components with single DCOM; drives standards via market saturation; brute-forces standards via volume of sales

metric_value: OEMs; Software Solution Suppliers (SSPs); Independent Professional Services Providers (IPSPs); EDI/VAN integrators
notes: Aberdeen taxonomy of EC professional services provider types; key analytical framework

metric_value: Revenue increase via new sales channel; competitive advantage; cost reduction via process efficiency; fear of being perceived as behind the times
notes: Based on IS manager interviews; four primary executive motivations for EC implementation

metric_value: Secure internet-based EC servers; sales capture/fulfillment automation; call center streamlining; supply chain streamlining
notes: Four implementation categories described by IS managers interviewed by Aberdeen

metric_value: Pronounced lack of skilled personnel; marketplace immaturity; solution immaturity (scalability/availability/security); real and perceived web stability concerns
notes: Aberdeen analysis of factors limiting EC acceptance; skill shortage as primary constraint
